response: The EU’s GDPR, effective May 2018, applies to any organization processing EU residents’ personal data—regardless of location—and grants rights to access, erasure, portability, explicit consent, mandatory Data Protection Officers for large‐scale processing, and 72-hour breach reporting. Violations can trigger fines of up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million (whichever is higher), with major penalties including Meta’s €1.2 billion fine in 2023, Amazon’s €746 million penalty, and Google’s over €200 million in fines. Inspired by GDPR, laws like California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act have emerged worldwide, though critics say compliance burdens stifle small businesses and innovation, enforcement varies, and cookie‐consent banners frustrate users. Despite these challenges, GDPR established data privacy as a fundamental right and set the global standard for data protection.
